- Call emergency services and/or police if not already done

**2. Observe and Document**
- Use all your senses to assess the scene
- Make mental notes immediately
- Record information in your notebook as time permits
- Note the time you arrived and secured the scene

**3. Protect the Scene**
- Remove curious onlookers
- Ask witnesses to step aside and wait
- Prevent people from walking through the scene
- Stop any attempts to clean up or disturb evidence
- Limit access by security personnel

### Transferring Authority to Police

When police arrive:
- Note their arrival time in your notebook
- Introduce yourself to the officer taking charge
- Answer all questions truthfully and completely
- Identify any witnesses present
- Obtain officer's name and contact information for your report

Documenting this transition demonstrates the scene remained secure with controlled access—critical information for court proceedings.

pages.title: Preparing for Court Testimony
pages.body:
## Preparing for Court Testimony

If you maintain accurate, complete notebooks and submit professional reports, preparing to testify in court should be manageable. Understanding the court process and proper preparation will help you fulfill this important professional responsibility confidently.

### The Subpoena

You will receive a **subpoena**—a formal document summoning you to testify about a particular matter. The subpoena specifies:
- Date and time of required attendance
- Which court and courtroom
- The matter you'll testify about

**Your attendance is mandatory.** Failure to attend may result in contempt of court charges. You can be called to court even after leaving your employer. Arrange time off with your supervisor if court falls during a scheduled shift.

### Pre-Court Preparation

**Review Your Documentation:**
- Identify the incident from the subpoena details
- Review your notebook entries thoroughly
- Read all reports you filed about the event
- Refresh your memory on specific details, timelines, and observations

**Arrive Early:**
- Allow extra time for parking
- Locate the correct courtroom in advance
- Avoid last-minute stress
- Re-read your notes before entering
- Speak with the Prosecutor if they wish to discuss your testimony

### Courtroom Best Practices

**Professional Appearance:**
- Wear clean, pressed uniform or professional attire
- Ensure you are well-groomed
- No gum chewing or beverages at the stand

**Communication Skills:**
- **Speak clearly and confidently**
- Use polite, respectful tone at all times
- Avoid sarcasm or humor
- Make eye contact with questioners
- Maintain good posture
- Use professional verbal and non-verbal communication

**Testimony Guidelines:**

**Always tell the truth.** This is paramount.

**State facts only**—no opinions or guesses.

**Answer only what is asked.** Sometimes "yes" or "no" suffices. If the lawyer wants more, they'll ask.

**Admit when you don't remember.** Saying "I don't know" is far better than guessing—it preserves your credibility as a witness.

**Refer to your notebook** for specific information when needed.

**Wait for the judge** if an objection is made. Don't continue speaking until the judge rules.

**Don't leave the stand** until the judge gives permission.

### Your Professional Advantage

As a security professional, you already possess skills for speaking professionally in unfamiliar situations. Court testimony simply applies these same capabilities in a formal legal setting.

pages.title: Alarm System Components and Functions
pages.body:
## Alarm System Components and Functions

An **alarm monitoring system** is a series of devices designed to warn occupants that an event outside of the "norm" is taking place. As a security professional, understanding how these systems work is essential for effective emergency response.

### Three Primary Components

Every alarm system consists of three key components working in sequence:

1. **Sensor** - Detects changes in the environment such as movement, light, temperature, smoke, or pressure
2. **Transmitter** - Sends the message from the sensor to the control panel using hard wires, telephone lines, radio signals, or wireless technology
3. **Control Panel** - Receives the information sent by the transmitter and initiates a response sequence

### Types of Alarms Monitored

Alarm systems may be used to detect or monitor:

- Fire (smoke)
- Intruders
- Temperature and humidity
- Toxic substances (e.g., carbon monoxide)
- Water pressure and water leaks
- Equipment operation

Some alarm systems anticipate emergency events while others monitor building operations. Most buildings with sophisticated operation systems employ personnel for monitoring, though during evening or weekend hours, an individual may be standing by "on call."

### Your Responsibilities

You should become familiar with the various types of alarm systems you encounter in your role. Knowing how to read and respond to the indicators provided by the alarm will help you be more effective. For older systems not equipped with automatic notifications, you must monitor for alarms on a regular basis and contact the appropriate person(s) at the first indication of a problem.

pages.title: Alarm Sensor Technologies
pages.body:
## Alarm Sensor Technologies

The **sensor** is the first component of an alarm system, detecting changes in the environment. Different types of sensors are used depending on the information being gathered. Understanding these technologies helps security professionals recognize how systems detect threats.

### Common Sensor Types

**Photo sensors** are responsive to light which is either visible or invisible. A common application involves monitors placed on entry doors where a beam of light crosses the threshold. When an individual enters through the door, the beam is broken, triggering a response.

**Ultrasonic sensors** work on the same principle as radar. The sensor emits a pulse which travels toward a target and returns to the source. The unit measures the amount of time it took for the pulse to return. If the return time becomes increasingly shorter, it indicates the target is moving closer to the sensor, causing an alarm response.

**Magnetic sensors** utilize magnets placed on two surfaces to create a magnetic field. For example, one magnet is placed on the moving part of a window and the other on the window frame. When the window is pushed open, the magnetic field is broken, initiating an alarm response.

**Shock sensors** represent an older technology utilizing a wire placed around the perimeter of a building opening. The wire forms a complete electrical circuit monitored by the alarm system. When the opening is breached, the wire is broken which interrupts the circuit and triggers the alarm.

**Fire alarms** may use different technologies to detect smoke and flame. Some use photo sensor technology where accumulated smoke breaks a light beam. Others use temperature sensors that activate when sudden temperature increases occur. Still others use ionization technology, monitoring air quality and triggering when it changes beyond a predetermined "normal" level.

**Gauges** are usually associated with building mechanical systems, monitoring functions like pressure or levels and indicating when readings are in normal or unsafe zones.

pages.title: Responding to Alarm Activations
pages.body:
## Responding to Alarm Activations

When an alarm is triggered, security professionals must respond appropriately according to established protocols. Your response must be immediate, systematic, and documented.

### Essential Knowledge Requirements

Make it your business to find out which alarms you are responsible for and the required response for each. You will need to know:

- Location of all alarms at your work site
- The subject of each alarm (what does it monitor?)
- Location of control panels
- Acceptable thresholds (levels) for building systems
- **Post orders** specific to your location

**Important**: Do not assume protocols will be the same even amongst locations operated by the same organization. Different buildings, facilities, and locations may call for different responses.

### Standard Response Protocol

When an alarm is triggered:

- **Respond to ALL alarms** - Always start with the assumption the alarm is genuine, even though false alarms are common
- **Contact your supervisor** to advise you are leaving your post and to report the alarm
- **Determine the source** of the alarm and the appropriate response
- For **false alarms** - follow the protocol provided in your post orders and report back to your supervisor
- For **emergencies** - follow the protocols provided in your post orders

### Managing Different Scenarios

Your scene management approach varies by situation:

- **Fires and other emergencies** - facilitate orderly evacuation of persons
- **Robbery or child abduction** - seal off exit doors
- **Building systems failure** - remain calm, advise persons to remain patient while the issue is resolved

### Off-Site Alarm Response

When responding to off-site alarms, comply with traffic laws - you may not exceed posted speed limits or ignore traffic signalling devices. Upon arrival, check that doors and windows are secure, noting any that appear open, unlocked, or otherwise unsecured. If you see evidence of a break-in or other criminal activity, notify the police immediately and take care not to displace or destroy evidence.

### Documentation Requirements

Document all events in your **notebook** and prepare reports as required, including any alarm shut-downs or testing actions.

pages.title: Emergency Scene Management Principles
pages.body:
## Emergency Scene Management Principles

When an emergency situation occurs, you will be looked upon to manage the safety and security of the scene. Your leadership during a crisis is critical to protecting persons and property.

### Core Professional Requirements

Regardless of the role you are required to play, **remain professional**. This means:

- **Be calm** - controlling your emotions helps others remain composed
- **Utilize professional communications skills** at all times
- **Remain continually observant** - you are still responsible for security of persons and property
- **Lead by example** - if you struggle to contain yourself, you will hinder emergency personnel efforts

You will likely be one of the first people to arrive on scene. Persons will turn to you for guidance and you need to be prepared to offer assistance.

### Types of Large-Scale Emergencies

The types of large-scale emergencies you can expect to deal with include:

- **Fires**
- **Bomb threats**

While major emergencies are not particularly frequent, their implications are rather large. You need to know now - before it happens - how to proceed.

### Evacuation Principles

Regardless of the type of emergency, principles for safe, efficient evacuation are generally the same:

1. **Remain calm and professional** at all times
2. **Do not shout** - speak louder if needed but avoid anxious yelling
3. **Identify safe emergency exits** from your earlier patrols
4. **Instruct persons** on the location of the nearest usable emergency exit
5. **Designate a meeting place** a safe distance from the building
6. **Instruct evacuees** to move swiftly and calmly, but not to run
7. **Make arrangements** for individuals with mobility needs or conditions which may hinder their escape

### Post-Emergency Responsibilities

When time allows, make appropriate entries in your **notebook**. Continue monitoring the crowd and remain alert for further instructions from your supervisor or investigators in charge. If approached by media, be professional but refer reporters to media professionals responsible for representing the police.

Watch for signs of shock or other health emergencies and call for emergency services at the first indication of a health crisis.

pages.title: Fire Emergency Procedures
pages.body:
## Fire Emergency Procedures

Fire is a common occurrence that can have significant impact on persons and property regardless of scale. Security professionals must know how to prevent fires, respond when they occur, and facilitate safe evacuations.

### Fire Prevention During Patrols

As part of your regular patrols, be constantly aware of fire hazards:

- Check that **fire equipment** (e.g., fire extinguishers) is in place and emergency exits are functional
- Become familiar with the location of **all emergency exits**, including stairwells and exterior fire escapes
- Verify fire escape doors can be opened and access from both sides is not blocked
- Familiarize yourself with **emergency escape plans**
- Identify individuals who may need assistance during emergencies (wheelchairs, health problems, visually or hearing impaired)
- Report hazards: overloaded plug sockets, garbage piles, space heaters near furniture or curtains
- Monitor that smokers use ashtrays properly
- Check for tampering with fire and smoke alarms
- Note stoves or hotplates left on unattended, or evidence of candle use

### When a Fire Occurs

If you discover a fire:

1. **Immediately activate the fire alarm**
2. **Check the area for occupants** and clear all persons from the building
3. **Close doors and windows** to keep fire from spreading
4. **Carry out directives** contained in your post orders

### Assessing Whether to Fight the Fire

Only after all persons are safely removed should you assess whether to attempt extinguishing the fire. Your decision should be based on:

- **Size of the fire** - only attempt to put out very small fires
- **Availability of appropriate equipment** and your training to use it
- **Presence of dangerous chemicals** - do NOT stay where fire and chemicals coexist
- **Presence of an escape route** if unsuccessful

### Fire Extinguisher Classes

**You must use a fire extinguisher rated for the type of fire:**

- **Class A** - Ordinary combustibles (paper, wood, fabric)
- **Class B** - Flammable and combustible liquids (fuel, oil, paint, grease)
- **Class C** - Electrical fire (wiring, fuse boxes)
- **Class D** - Metals (flammable metals such as magnesium and sodium)

### PASS Method

To use a fire extinguisher:

- **P**ull the pin or remove the safety catch
- **A**im the nozzle at the base of the fire
- **S**queeze or press the handle
- **S**weep the nozzle from side to side

pages.title: Bomb Threat Response Protocol
pages.body:
## Bomb Threat Response Protocol

A **bomb threat** is notification about the alleged presence of an explosive device at your location. While uncommon and often a hoax, you must treat every bomb threat as a real and very serious situation.

### Why Bomb Threats Occur

Potential reasons for bomb threats include:

- **Political statement** - protests of particular political positions
- **Revenge** - creating chaos and disruption for the target organization
- **Eco-terrorism** - targeting industries that disrupt the environment
- **Activism** - special interest groups capitalizing on attention

Some individuals making threats may actually want to warn about genuine danger without revealing their identity or connection to the threat.

### Responding to Telephone Bomb Threats

Guidelines for dealing with a caller making a bomb threat:

- **Listen** - remain calm and be polite
- **Do not interrupt** the caller
- **Obtain as much information as possible** using the bomb threat checklist
- **Start a call trace** while still on the phone (if possible)
- **Notify your partner or supervisor** and request police be called
- **Complete the bomb threat checklist** and submit it to your supervisor immediately

### Key Information to Gather

The **bomb threat checklist** should capture:

- When will the bomb explode?
- Where is the bomb located?
- What does the bomb look like?
- Where is the caller calling from?
- Why has the bomb been placed?
- Caller's name (if provided)
- Caller details: sex, approximate age, accent, voice characteristics, speech patterns, manner
- Background noises heard
- Whether voice is familiar
- Whether caller appears familiar with the area

### After the Call

Record details in your **notebook**: date, time, and exact wording of the threat. **Do not speak to anyone else about the call** - this might incite panic.

### Written Bomb Threats

Alert your supervisor and authorities immediately. Keep any packaging (envelope, box) to turn over to police.

### Next Steps

Your job is to **report the bomb threat** to your supervisors and/or police according to **post orders**. Continue regular duties with additional vigilance, watching for suspicious items or behaviours. The decision whether to ignore, search, or evacuate must come from someone other than yourself.

### If Called to Search

If you find something suspicious:

1. **Do not touch it!**
2. **Do not move or change anything**
3. **Do not use your phone or radio** (electronic devices can detonate bombs) - move at least 50 metres away first
4. **Move out slowly** and secure access
5. **Report findings** with exact location details
6. **Stay alert** for further instructions

pages.title: Personal Safety Fundamentals for Security Professionals
pages.body:
## Personal Safety Fundamentals for Security Professionals

Personal safety begins with your own **attitude and preparedness** for the role as a security professional. Your physical and mental fitness are crucial components of personal safety even before you step foot on the work site.

### Self-Assessment Before Every Shift

Use the same method of **observing and assessing** to determine whether you are fit to perform your duties prior to beginning every shift. If you are overtired, ill, or otherwise incapacitated, you are:

- A hazard to yourself
- A hazard to the persons you are supposed to be protecting
- A liability to your employer

### Wellness and Fitness Requirements

Be conscientious about wellness by:

- **Getting enough sleep** to meet your needs
- **Eating a healthy, balanced diet**
- **Addressing medical concerns** with a health professional
- **Participating in activities** that build and maintain your fitness level

There is always a physical requirement of security professionals, even if you work a stationary posting most of the time. Your level of fitness contributes to your ability to **combat fatigue** and can help keep your **cognitive function and powers of observation sharp**.

### Proper Equipment and Uniform Maintenance

Always wear and use any **safety or protective equipment** as required by your employer. Items such as steel-toed boots and reflective vests help ensure your safety in the presence of hazards. Keep your uniform in good repair—tears in fabric or flaps of cloth can get caught in equipment or machinery, leading to injury.

### Weather-Appropriate Protection

- Wear a **jacket when patrolling outdoors in cold weather** to protect against hypothermia or frostbite
- Use a **water repellent coat during periods of rain**
- Apply **sunscreen and wear a hat** when patrolling outdoors during the day, in both winter and summer

pages.title: Communication and Safety Protocols
pages.body:
## Communication and Safety Protocols

Developing effective communication habits is essential for maintaining personal safety while on duty as a security professional. Regular communication with co-workers or supervisors serves multiple protective functions.

### Establishing Communication Patterns

Develop a habit of **communicating on a regular basis** while on duty. Check in:

- At the **beginning of your shift**
- **Before and after breaks**
- At the **end of your shift**

This practice lets others know you are at your post and serves to **benchmark (establish a standard)** your communication patterns. You may call more frequently if time and circumstances permit. Your employer is partially responsible for ensuring your safety and will appreciate your checking in regularly.

### Communication During Patrols

You should also communicate with others during the course of your patrols. This may include:

- Individuals employed by the same organization
- Persons who work at various locations within the site

For example, it may become your habit to stop at the information desk each time you patrol the lobby area.

### Benefits of Regular Contact

There are at least two important benefits to regular contact:

1. **You have an opportunity to ask** if there are any concerns you should be made aware of
2. **The individual(s) will come to expect your presence** and more importantly, notice when you do not stop by, which may prompt them to have someone check up on you

### Emergency Response Communication

Maintaining **professional composure by staying calm** provides the most reassurance to individuals at the scene during emergencies. Your own safety is paramount during times of crisis; if you are injured or otherwise incapacitated you will be unable to perform your duties and assist others in their efforts to get to safety.

pages.title: Risk Assessment at Security Sites
pages.body:
## Risk Assessment at Security Sites

Throughout your work as a security professional, you will encounter various situations, but one thing common to all sites is the existence of an **associated level of risk**. Understanding how to assess and categorize risk is fundamental to maintaining personal safety.

### Three Categories of Risk

For security duties, consider three broad categories:

**Low Risk Settings:**
- Day shift
- Posting in a non-hazardous location (e.g., lobby of an office tower)

**Medium Risk Settings:**
- Evening shift
- Parking lots during daylight hours
- Construction or industrial site during daylight hours
- Venues with large numbers of persons present

**High Risk Settings:**
- Night shift
- Working in area with high crime level
- Working around large amounts of cash or valuable goods
- Working in settings where there is a risk of violence (e.g., labour dispute)
- Parking lot in darkness
- Construction or industrial site during hours of darkness
- Isolated location
- Areas with limited radio or cell phone signal

### Comprehensive Risk Assessment Factors

You will need to assess the risk at each site you work. Other factors to consider include:

- **The potential for a risky event to occur** (e.g., how likely are you to encounter a fire)
- **How frequently does the risk happen** (e.g., how often do you need to remove trespassers)

### Risk Elevation Through Multiple Factors

You might be posted at a medium-risk setting, such as a parking lot during daylight hours, but because it is in an isolated location and popular for trespassers, the regular frequency with which you interact with them elevates this setting into a **potential high risk situation**.

A constant and systematic reassessment of site hazards is a **proactive approach** to maintaining your personal safety.

pages.title: Identifying Workplace Hazards
pages.body:
## Identifying Workplace Hazards

The Canadian Centre for Occupational Health and Safety (CCOHS) defines a **hazard** as "any source of potential damage, harm or adverse health effects on something or someone under certain conditions at work."

### Categories of Workplace Hazards

CCOHS identifies several categories with potential harm:

- **Thing:** Scaffolding (collapse)
- **Substance:** Smoke (smoke inhalation, death)
- **Material:** Asbestos (cancer)
- **Energy source:** Electricity (electric shock)
- **Condition:** Icy surfaces (slip and fall)
- **Practice:** Working at height without safety rope (falling)
- **Process:** Chemical transport (accidental tank puncture leading to spillage or explosion)

### Uncontrolled Release of Energy

Hazards also include uncontrolled release of energy:

- Object falling from a height (**gravitational energy**)
- Out of control chemical reaction (**chemical energy**)
- Release of pressurized gas or steam (**pressure**)
- Clothing or hair becoming tangled in moving machinery (**kinetic energy**)
- Contact with a fallen power line (**electrical energy**)

### Hazards at Security Postings

Common hazards you may encounter include:

- Isolated conditions
- Traffic (particularly in parking lots or when doing traffic control)
- Weather (icy surfaces, cold temperatures, excessive heat)
- Angry or uncooperative persons
- Unfinished structures (construction sites)
- Crowds
- Alcohol sales

### Comprehensive Hazard Identification Process

CCOHS suggests:

- **Examine all aspects of the work**, including non-routine activities such as maintenance, repair, or cleaning
- **Review accident/incident/near-miss records**
- **Study how work is organized** including experience and age of workers
- **Consider foreseeable unusual conditions** such as power outages
- **Examine risks to visitors or the public**
- **Perform an assessment of groups** which may have a different level of risk

**Hazard assessment should be an ongoing process**—you cannot predict which hazards will remain the same from day to day.

pages.title: Managing Health During Shift Work
pages.body:
## Managing Health During Shift Work

Security patrols are often required during evening and night time hours, leading to the need for **shift work** amongst security professionals. Some of the most common negative effects include disruption of the **circadian (sleep) rhythm**, sleep deprivation, disorders of the gastrointestinal and cardiovascular systems, worsening of existing disorders, and disruption of family and social life.

### Health and Nutrition Strategies

Shift work disrupts normal social patterns and routines, such as meal time. The Occupational Health Clinics for Ontario Workers recommend:

- **Afternoon workers** should have their meal in the middle of the day instead of the middle of their work shift
- **Night workers** should eat lightly throughout the shift and have a moderate breakfast
- Relax during meals and allow time for digestion
- Drink lots of water
- Cut back on highly salted foods and reduce foods high in fat
- Maintain **regular eating patterns with well-balanced meals** (avoid junk food)
- Eat the usual balance of vegetables, fruit, lean meat, poultry, fish, dairy products, grains, and bread
- Avoid excessive use of antacids, tranquilizers and sleeping pills
- Minimize the intake of **caffeine and alcohol**
- Avoid fast food and vending machines

### Physical Fitness During Shift Work

Fitness contributes to your ability to remain **focused and alert** on the job. Being physically fit aids your energy levels which sustain you through long shifts. Healthy U suggests:

- Try to **exercise during breaks**
- Talk with co-workers while you work
- Try to work with a **"buddy"**
- Take short breaks throughout your shift to use the employee lounge, take a walk, shoot hoops in the parking lot, or climb stairs
- Don't leave the most tedious or boring tasks to the end of your shift when you will feel most sleepy
- Exchange ideas with colleagues on ways to cope
- **Avoid exercising before going to bed**—allow three hours to pass between exercising and going to sleep

pages.title: Sleep Management for Shift Workers
pages.body:
## Sleep Management for Shift Workers

The most obvious affect shift work has on your physical and mental well-being is the **disruption to your regular sleep patterns**. Despite your family's best attempts to ensure you are not disturbed and your own good intentions, it is not an easy task to obtain a quality sleep during daylight hours when most of the world is awake.

### Strategies for Better Sleep

The following suggestions may help you get the rest your body needs:

- **Make sure your family and friends are aware** of and considerate of your sleep hours and needs
- Ensure you have a **comfortable, quiet place to sleep** during the day
- Use devices that may improve your sleep: air conditioning, telephone answering machines, ear plugs and good window coverings
- **Make time for quiet relaxation before bed** to facilitate better sleep (reading, breathing exercises, muscle relaxation techniques)
- **Sleep on a set schedule** to help establish a routine and to make sleep during the day easier
- **Avoid strenuous exercise before sleeping** as your body's metabolism will remain elevated for several hours afterward, making sleep difficult
- If you do not fall asleep after one hour, read a book or listen to quiet music
- If sleep still does not come, **reschedule your sleeping hours** for later in the day

### Finding What Works

You will probably go through a **process of trial and error** until you discover what works best for you, but it will be worth it to get the rest you need. You will:

- Stay healthier
- Feel better
- Have more energy for your work and personal life

### Impact on Alertness

Quality sleep is essential for maintaining the **cognitive function and powers of observation** necessary for security work. Sleep deprivation can compromise your ability to perform your duties effectively and safely.

pages.title: Maintaining Social Life During Shift Work
pages.body:
## Maintaining Social Life During Shift Work

Working shifts wreaks havoc with your social life. Even if you are not a person who typically goes out often you will still notice the way shift work infringes upon your personal or relaxation time. All of us require the balance brought to us by our **"down time"** and the freedom to choose what we want to do.

### Impact of Isolation

Extended periods of working evening or night shift can cause you to **lose contact with family and friends** and lead to a feeling of isolation. This isolation can compound the other negative effects of shift work and impact your overall well-being.

### Strategies for Maintaining Social Connections

The Occupational Health Clinics for Ontario Workers provide the following ideas for maintaining a social life while being engaged in shift work:

- **Schedule at least one daily meal with your family**—this helps to keep communication channels open and promotes good eating habits
- **Socialize with other shift workers and their families**—this helps to minimize the disruption that shift work can have on your social life
- Keep in touch with your spouse/partner and children daily
- **Set time aside for just you and your spouse/partner**
- **Carefully plan family activities**—family ties are a precious commodity (plan days off in advance if possible)

### Stress Management and Organization

Additional strategies include:

- Practice **stress reduction**
- **Use a calendar to schedule events**
- Try to **prioritize tasks and tackle one at a time**

### The Challenge and the Reward

Working shift work will cause you challenges but working to implement the strategies and ideas should help to ease your concerns and protect your **health and well being both on and off the job**. Maintaining social connections is not just about personal happiness—it directly impacts your effectiveness and safety as a security professional.
